coredata.dc:title: Marcasite-FeS2@carbon nanodots anchored on 3D cell-like graphenic matrix for high-rate and ultrastable potassium ion storage
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Kun
Zhao, Wang
Yu, Qiyao
Liu, Zhiwei
Li, Ping
Wang, Wei (Alex)
Song, Lei
An, Fuqiang
Cao, Peng
Qu, Xuanhui
coredata.dc:description: Marcasite-FeS2 has significant potential as an anode material for potassium-ion batteries (KIBs) for its lower band gap, high theoretical capacity, low cost and environmental friendliness but suffers poor cycle stability and rate capability. Here, we design a marcasite-FeS2@carbon nanodots anchored on 3D cell-like S, N co-doped graphenic matrix (m-FeS2@C-SSNFG). The 3D graphenic matrix is constructed by graphenic cages and sustained by conductive graphitic struts that can curb the agglomeration of graphenic sheets during cycling. Remarkably, the yolk-shell m-FeS2@graphenic cage structure not only ensures uniform dispersion of m-FeS2 on the cell wall, but also offers enough void room to accommodate the volume expansion of m-FeS2. Meanwhile the graphitic carbon shell wrapped around m-FeS2 also prevents the active m-FeS2 nanodots from agglomeration and dissolving into electrolyte. It is worth underlining that the phase transition from semiconductor (m-FeS2) to conductor (KxFeS2) is firstly validated through first-principles calculations, beneficial to the electron transfer and K-ions diffusion in subsequent cycles. Besides, optimizing electrolyte and cut-off voltage can further boost its long-term cycle stability. Thus, it delivers high capacity of 419 mA h g−1 after 100 cycles at 0.05 A g−1 and ultralong cycle stability of 140 mA h g−1 at 5 A g−1 over 4000 cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Marcasite-FeS2
Phase transition
Ether-based electrolyte
High cut-off voltage
Potassium-ion battery

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the fabrication of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG.

Phase and morphology characterization. (a) SEM image of Fe3C@C-SNFG. (b–d) SEM images, (e) AFM image and the corresponding line scans analysis (inset), and (f, g) TEM images of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG. (h) HRTEM image of m-FeS2 nanoparticles wrapped by graphite carbon. (i) HAADF-STEM image and (j) the corresponding elemental mappings of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG. (k) XRD patterns of Fe3C@C-SNFG and m-FeS2@C-SSNFG.

Structural characterization of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG. (a) Crystal structure of marcasite-FeS2. (b) Raman spectrum. (c) C 1s XPS spectrum. (d) S 2p XPS spectrum. (e) Fe 2p XPS spectrum. (f) N 1s XPS spectrum. (g) Schematic illustration of the nitrogen and sulfur dual-doping in m-FeS2@C-SSNFG. (h) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms, and (i) pore size distribution.

Electrochemical Performance of marcasite-FeS2-based Anode for KIBs in a voltage range of 0.8–3.0 V when using the electrolyte of KFSI in DME. (a) Discharge/charge profiles of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG for the different cycles at 0.05 A g−1 (the inset of Fig. 4a is the first discharge/charge profiles). (b) Cycle stability of m-FeS2, and m-FeS2@C-SSNFG at 0.05 A g−1. (c) Rate capability of m-FeS2, and m-FeS2@C-SSNFG at varied current densities from 0.05 to 5 A g−1. (d) Comparison of rate capability between m-FeS2@C-SSNFG and the as-reported anode materials for KIBs. (e) Long-term cycle stability of m-FeS2, and m-FeS2@C-SSNFG at high current densities of 2 A g−1 for 1000 cycles.

The effect of electrolytes and cutting-off voltage on cycle stability of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode. (a) KFSI and (b) KPF6 as the K salts with the solvents of EC/DEC or DME. (c) Long-term cycle stability in different voltage range at 5 A g−1 when using the electrolyte of KFSI in DME. (d–k) HRTEM images and the corresponding SAED patterns of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode after initial discharge to (d, e) 0.01 V, (f, g) 0.5 V, (h, i) 0.8 V, and (j, k) after initial charge from 0.8 to 3.0 V.

Theoretical calculations of band structure, density of states (DOS) and corresponding optimized crystal structure. (a, e and f) Marcasite FeS2. (b, g and h) K0.5FeS2. (c, i and j) KFeS2. (d, k and l) K1.5FeS2. The purple, brown and yellow balls represent K, Fe and S atoms, respectively. The Fermi levels are set to zero and are indicated by the dashed lines. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

K ion diffusion paths (green arrows) in the supercells and corresponding energy variations and energy barriers of KxFeS2. (a–d) Optimized supercells and K ion diffusion paths of marcasite FeS2, K0.5FeS2, KFeS2, K1.5FeS2. (e–g) Energy variations and energy barriers for K ion diffusion in (e) marcasite FeS2 and K0.5FeS2, (f) KFeS2, and (g) K1.5FeS2. The purple, brown and yellow balls represent K and S atoms, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Marcasite-FeS2@carbon nanodots anchored on 3D cell-like graphenic matrix for high-rate and ultrastable potassium ion storage

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Marcasite-FeS2 has significant potential as an anode material for potassium-ion batteries (KIBs) for its lower band gap, high theoretical capacity, low cost and environmental friendliness but suffers poor cycle stability and rate capability. Here, we design a marcasite-FeS2@carbon nanodots anchored on 3D cell-like S, N co-doped graphenic matrix (m-FeS2@C-SSNFG). The 3D graphenic matrix is constructed by graphenic cages and sustained by conductive graphitic struts that can curb the agglomeration of graphenic sheets during cycling. Remarkably, the yolk-shell m-FeS2@graphenic cage structure not only ensures uniform dispersion of m-FeS2 on the cell wall, but also offers enough void room to accommodate the volume expansion of m-FeS2. Meanwhile the graphitic carbon shell wrapped around m-FeS2 also prevents the active m-FeS2 nanodots from agglomeration and dissolving into electrolyte. It is worth underlining that the phase transition from semiconductor (m-FeS2) to conductor (KxFeS2) is firstly validated through first-principles calculations, beneficial to the electron transfer and K-ions diffusion in subsequent cycles. Besides, optimizing electrolyte and cut-off voltage can further boost its long-term cycle stability. Thus, it delivers high capacity of 419 mA h g−1 after 100 cycles at 0.05 A g−1 and ultralong cycle stability of 140 mA h g−1 at 5 A g−1 over 4000 cycles.

ce:section.ce:para: The ever-increasing and foreseen demand for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) that cover various applications ranging from consumer electronics to transportation and large-scale smart grids has raised fear of lithium shortage and driven researchers to find more sustainable battery technologies based on earth-abundant elements. Sustainability wise, two appealing alternatives to LIBs are Na-ion batteries (NIBs) and K-ion batteries (KIBs) due to the similar chemical properties and high abundance and low cost of raw materials [1–5]. Compared with NIBs, utilizing the heavier K-ions as charge carriers may be not highly competitive owing to the increase in weight, however, the entire mass of the battery is truly not significant for stationary energy storage purposes where the energy density is not the priority [6]. Furthermore, KIBs can be expected and will be more advantageous because of higher voltage derived from intrinsic lower redox potential (−2.93 V for K+/K and −2.71 V for Na+/Na) and faster kinetics owing to the weaker Lewis acidity of K+ in liquid electrolyte, making KIBs an promising candidate to substitute LIBs in large-scale smart grids [6,7]. Nevertheless, the large volume expansion and high mechanical stress/strain upon electrochemical cycling remain a great challenge due to the large ionic radius of K+ (1.38 Å), leading to poor cycling performance.

Against this background, significant efforts have been made to look for suitable electrode materials that can offer enough room to buffer the dramatic volume changes. In terms of anode materials for KIBs, although carbon-based electrode materials exhibit outstanding cycle stability, even at high current density their low storage capacity is still a fatal drawback [8–11]. Considering the high theoretical capacity of conversion-type metal sulfides anode materials, especially the FeS2, that is an exceptional candidate due to its high storage capacity, cheapness and environmental beingness. Normally, there are two distinct polymorphs of FeS2, including pyrite FeS2 (p-FeS2) and marcasite FeS2 (m-FeS2). It is worth pointing out that m-FeS2 shows a much lower semiconducting band gap (0.4 eV) than that of p-FeS2 (0.7 eV) according to previous reports, meaning a higher electronic conductivity of m-FeS2 [12,13]. Additionally, due to the stronger Fe–S bonds and the weaker S–S interactions in m-FeS2 than those of the p-FeS2 [14], the m-FeS2 exhibits theoretically a better electrochemical performance than p-FeS2. Despite all this, the m-FeS2 is difficult to synthesize due to its low stability of metastable nature. Moreover, the intrinsic issue of conversion-type anode materials included m-FeS2 still exists, that is, the poor cycle stability caused by the large volume variations and the poor rate capability due to the semiconductor nature. It is generally acknowledged that the combination of carbon matrix such as heteroatom doping graphenic materials [15] and metal sulfides anode nanomaterials is an effective strategy to improve the electrochemical performance of KIBs [16–18]. For instance, Guan et al. reported that the graphene/p-FeS2 composite can exhibit a high reversible capacity and good rate capability as KIBs anode, and the potassium storage mechanism contains an intercalation reaction with an intermediate product of KxFeS2 followed by a conversion reaction with a final product of Fe and K2S [19]. However, the long-term stability is still not satisfactory enough at high current densities, which is closely related to the conversion-type reaction in FeS2 and the inevitable stacking of 2D graphene sheet during cycling. Notably, the conversion reaction brings more severe volume expansion to electrode materials, triggering poor cycle stability. In addition, the low chemical stability of commonly used carbonate-based electrolytes for FeS2-based electrode materials also leads to the bad cycling performance due to the side reactions between the electrolytes and anionic group [20]. Drawing on successful knowledge from LIBs and NIBs, controlling the discharge cut-off voltage to curb conversion reactions and using ether-based electrolytes to prohibit the sides reactions and form a stable solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) have been proved to be an efficient method to enhance the cycle stability of metal sulfides anode materials [21,22]. Thus, it would achieve a high-performance KIBs anode by rationally designing a 3D graphene matrix with special microstructures, using the ether-based electrolytes with highly chemical stability and avoiding the detrimental conversion reaction by adjusting the cut-off voltage.

Herein, we for the first time synthesized a 3D strutted cell-like S, N co-doped graphenic matrix decorated with marcasite-FeS2@carbon nanodots (denoted as m-FeS2@C-SSNFG) and investigate the effects of microstructure of graphenic matrix, ether-based electrolytes and discharge cut-off voltage on the potassium storage performance of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG as an excellent anode for KIBs. The m-FeS2@C-SSNFG is composed of countless interconnected graphenic cages that are constructed by S, N co-doped few-layer graphenic (~1.35 nm) walls decorated with marcasite-FeS2@C nanodots and the graphenic cages are spatially strutted by the graphitic struts. The graphitic struts can not only curb the agglomeration of graphenic sheets, but offer good electronic conductivity functioned as an 3D electronic highway. Specially, the m-FeS2@C nanodots tightly anchored onto the walls of graphenic cage and the single graphenic cage can form a wonderful yolk-shell structure, which ensures the evenly distribution of m-FeS2 and offers enough space to accommodate the volume expansion of m-FeS2 upon cycling, improving the cycle stability. Meantime, the graphitic carbon shells can fix the active m-FeS2 so as not to agglomerate and dissolve into electrolyte. Remarkably, the semiconductive m-FeS2 turns into conductive KxFeS2 after first charge process, enhancing the electronic conductivity and K ion diffusion during following cycles. We also demonstrate that adopting the ether-based electrolytes of 1 M KFSI in DME and tailoring the discharge cut-off voltage of 0.8 V can further boost the cycle stability. Hence, the m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode displays good rate capability (233 and 186 mA h g−1 at 2 and 5 A g−1), and exceptional cycle stability with a high capacity of 419 mA h g−1 at 0.05 A g−1 as well as an impressively ultralong cycle stability up to 4000 cycles even at a high current density of 5 A g−1. Additionally, our work may offer comprehensions of materials engineering strategies to enhance the electrochemical properties of metal sulfides anodes for KIBs.

Synthesis of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG: The m-FeS2@C-SSNFG was fabricated through a chemical blowing process and a sulfidation procedure. Firstly, 1.2 g polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP, K13) was dissolved into 40 ml deionized water at room temperature to form a transparent solution, and 3.5 mmol Fe(NO3)3·9H2O was added to above transparent solution under stirring and ultrasonication to form a yellow-brown solution. The obtained mixed solution was dried completely at 70 °C, and further ground and annealed at 800 °C for 1.5 h in Ar/H2 flow with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. Then, a foam-like product of the Fe3C@C-SNFG was collected. Secondly, the as-synthesized Fe3C@C-SNFG and sublimed sulfur with a mass ratio of 1:5 were placed at two separate locations in one quartz crucible, and the sublimed sulfur was placed at the upstream side of the furnace. Then, the crucible was heated at 300 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 1 °C min−1 in static Ar flow (100 sccm). Finally, the foam-like m-FeS2@C-SSNFG was obtained after cooling to room temperature. For comparison, the foam-like pyrite-FeS2@C-SSNFG anode material was prepared by sulfurizing the Fe3C@C-SNFG precursor at 500 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 in static Ar flow (100 sccm).

Synthesis of SSNFG: Appropriate amount of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG was dispersed in HCl solution (2 mol L−1) under stirring for 12 h at 80 °C, followed by cooling to room temperature naturally. Then, the precipitate was washed with deionized water and dried in vacuum oven at 80 °C. Finally, the foam-like SSNFG was collected.

Synthesis of m-FeS2: Typically, 0.404 g Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and 0.6508 g L-cysteine were dissolved into 20 ml deionized water under stirring for 20 min to form a black emulsion, followed by added 20 ml diethylene glycol under stirring for 1 h at temperature. Then, the mixed solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave of 50 ml capacity. The autoclave was sealed and kept at 185 °C for 50 h in an electronic oven and then was cooled to room temperature naturally. The final product was collected by centrifugation, washed with deionized water and ethanol and finally dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 12 h.

The morphology of the synthesized samples was studied by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi SU8010), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2010) with selected-area electron diffraction (SAED), and atomic force microscopy (AFM, JEOL JSPM-5200). The crystal structures and chemistry information of the as-prepared samples were examined by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, attachments of JEOL JEM-2010), XRD (Bruker D2 Phaser X-Ray Diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation), and Raman spectroscopy (a Kr–Ar ion laser at 633 nm produced by Spectra-Physics Beamlok 2060-RS laser combining Symphony CCD-1LS detection system). The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were characterized by a Quantachrome Autosorb AS-6B system. The thermal analysis was investigated by thermogravimetric (Rigaku Thermo plus TG 8120) methods and differential scanning calorimetry (Rigaku Thermo plus EVO DSC8230). The element content and chemical state of different elements was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250 Xi).

The working electrodes were fabricated using a slurry-coating method. The active materials were mixed with Super P and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) in a mass ratio of 8: 1: 1. N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) was used as the solvent. The slurry was coated on copper foil and dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 8 h. Then, the coated foil was punched into pellets with a diameter of 10 mm, and the mass loading of the pellets is ca. 1.2 mg cm−2 (Fig. S24). Coin-type cells (CR2032) were assembled in an argon-filled glovebox with potassium metal as the counter electrode, 1 M KFSI in DME or 1 M KPF6 in DME or 1 M KFSI in EC/DEC or 0.8 M KPF6 in EC/DEC (1:1 by volume) as electrolytes, a glass-fiber as a separator, and working electrodes. The galvanostatic charge/discharge tests were performed using a LAND CT2001A battery tester between 0.8–3.0 V versus K/K+ (0.01–3.0 V, 0.1–3.0 V, and 0.5–3.0 V for comparison purposes). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were carried out on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation over the sweep rate range from 0.1 mV s−1 to 2 mV s−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation over the frequency range from 0.01 to 105 Hz (a.c. amplification voltage: 5 mV).

All atomic and electronic structure calculations were carried out by using the projector augmented wave (PAW) method [23] in the framework of density functional theory [24], as implemented in the Vienna ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP). The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) [25] and Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) exchange functional [24] was used. The plane-wave energy cutoff is set to 500 eV, and the Monkhorst–Pack method [26] with 3 × 2 × 3, 1 × 1 × 1, 2 × 1 × 2 and 2 × 1 × 1 k-points mesh were employed for the geometry optimizations of the unit cells of FeS2, K0.5FeS2, KFeS2 and K1.5FeS2, respectively. The electronic structure calculations (density of electron states) of FeS2, K0.5FeS2, KFeS2 and K1.5FeS2 unit cells used the denser k-points meshes of 6 × 4 × 6, 2 × 2 × 2, 4 × 2 × 4 and 4 × 2 × 2, respectively. The convergence criterions of energy and force calculations are set to 10−5 eV/atom and 0.01 eV Å−1, respectively. Energy barriers of potassium ion diffusion were calculated by using the climbing image nudged elastic band (CI-NEB) method [27] and based on the supercell models, which has been successfully applied for the research of on alkali metal ion diffusion in battery materials [28–30].

The m-FeS2@C-SSNFG was fabricated through a facile chemical blowing process and a sulfidation procedure, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . Firstly, the Fe3C@C-SNFG was synthesized by a polymer chemical blowing technique. Specifically, a molten fluid of the mixture of polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) and Fe(NO3)3 was first blown up into countless polymer cages by tremendous volume gases chemically released from the thermal-induced decomposition of inner Fe(NO3)3. Then, the polymer cages were continuously expanded by the gas pressure and blowing, leading to the ultrathin cage walls [31]. And the cage walls were subsequently converted to ultrathin few-layer graphenic membranes by the carbonization at high temperature, yielding numerous connected graphenic cages. Meanwhile, Fe3C nanodots were formed through carbothermal reduction of the decomposition residues of Fe(NO3)3 and tightly anchored onto the cage walls. Furthermore, the graphite carbon shells wrapped around Fe3C grown along the facets of Fe3C nanodots via a vacancy mechanism of self-diffusion [32]. Finally, a sulfidation treatment was conducted on the as-prepared Fe3C@C-SNFG using sublimed sulfur to synthesize m-FeS2@C-SSNFG.

Fig. 2 a shows the Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of the honeycomb-like architecture of Fe3C@C-SNFG packed by numerous graphenic cages connected to each other, and these cages are sustained firmly by the graphitic struts that is denoted as the borders where several graphenic cages merged [33]. The cage walls are made up of cross-linked few-layer graphenic membranes with a thickness of ca. 1.35 nm (Fig. S1), that is, four-layer graphene, according to Atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis. Fig. S2a further reveals the overall panoramas of foam-like Fe3C@C-SNFG, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image shows the interconnected graphitic struts are integrated with graphene cages to frame a unique architecture (Fig. S2b). Additionally, the Fe3C@C nanodots are uniformly distributed on the cage walls and the graphitic carbon shells surrounded Fe3C@C nanodots are also observed due to the catalytic effect of Fe3C nanodots [32] (Fig. S2c, d and S3).

As expected, the honeycomb-like framework of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG is perfectly inherited even after sulfidation treatment (Fig. 2b and c). Fig. 2d shows pieces of walls of the graphene cages belonged to m-FeS2@C-SSNFG, which displays the interconnected graphitic struts. AFM analysis result is also consistent with the Fe3C@C-SNFG (Fig. 2e). Moreover, we can clearly observe that the graphenic cages are conformed with interconnected graphitic struts (Fig. 2f) which play critical roles of a conductive network to transfer electrons and a support to curb the agglomeration of graphenic sheets during cycling. Besides, the m-FeS2@C nanodots (Fig. 2g) and the surrounding graphitic carbon shells (inset of Fig. 2g) are evenly anchored on the cage walls. A lattice spacings of 0.24 nm and 0.34 nm, corresponding to the (111) plane of m-FeS2 and the (002) plane of graphite respectively, are identified by the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2h). The high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) image (Fig. 2i) and corresponding elemental mapping (Fig. 2j) of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG visualize the homogenous distribution of C, N, Fe and S. The crystal structure of Fe3C@C-SNFG and m-FeS2@C-SSNFG were further characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), which is in agreement with the phase of Fe3C (JCPDS No. 35–0772) and the orthorhombic crystal phase of m-FeS2 (JCPDS No. 88–2282) (Fig. 2k), respectively. And the enlarged area is underlined by the side of XRD figure, showing a diffraction peaks at 26.2° indexed to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon. It is worth pointing that the m-FeS2 nanodots anchored onto the cage walls and the graphenic cage constitute a yolk-shell structure, which can offer enough room to buffer the volume expansion of m-FeS2 upon cycling, favoring the cycle stability.

For comparison, the SSNFG decorated with hollow carbon shells was synthesized by etching the m-FeS2 nanodots away. The efficient removal of m-FeS2 nanodots remains plentiful hollow carbon shells on SSNFG and keeps the completely honeycomb-like structure, as confirmed by SEM, TEM and XRD analysis (Figs. S4 and S5). In addition, Figs. S6 and S7 show the morphology and the crystal structure of m-FeS2 nanoparticles, which displays a morphology of particle agglomeration. Conversely, with the 3D graphenic matrix constructed by the microstructures of graphenic cages and graphitic carbon shells, the m-FeS2 can be uniformly dispersed and firmly fixed on the cage walls, avoiding the dissolution into electrolyte of m-FeS2.

Fig. 3 a displays a unit cell of m-FeS2, where the Fe–S interactions are edge-sharing and the Fe atoms are octahedrally coordinated with six S atoms. The Raman spectrum (Fig. 3b) of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG shows three peaks at around 282 cm−1, 320 cm−1 and 385 cm−1, corresponding to the semiconductive m-FeS2 [34], and another three strong peaks at around 1328 cm−1, 1585 cm−1 and 2685 cm−1 correspond to the disordered carbon (D-band), crystalline graphitic carbon (G-band) and the few-layer graphenic membranes structure [35]. The intensity ratio of I
D
/I
G is ca. 1.14, implying the domination of the defective structure in 3D graphenic matrix caused by the N, S co-doping which derives from the inherent N in PVP and the sulfidation treatment, respectively. The surface chemistry of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG was further examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) exhibits the peaks at around 284.8, 285.6, 286.5 and 289.0 eV, corresponding to C–C, CN, C–O and O–CO groups, respectively [36]. The CN bond can be assigned to the N doping which is beneficial to introduce plentiful defects and more active sites, enhancing the potassium storage performance of the electrode [37]. For the hybrid of the m-FeS2@C-SSNFG, the two major peaks at around 162.4 and 163.5 eV of the high-resolution S 2p spectrum are doublet structures owing to spin-orbit coupling in m-FeS2 (Fig. 3d) [34]. Besides, another peak at around 164.2 eV could be ascribed to the C–S–C 2p3/2 bond, which corresponds to the highly-conductive thiophenic S structure (Fig. 3d) [38]. The high-resolution Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 3e) displays two dominant peaks at around 707.5 and 720.5 eV, corresponding to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 doublet splitting characteristic due to spin-orbit coupling in m-FeS2 [39]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3f) shows the presence of three types of N, with the peaks at around 398.7, 399.5 and 400.9 eV, corresponding to the pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively [40]. Schematic illustration of the three forms of N defects and S defects doped into the m-FeS2@C-SSNFG is shown in Fig. 3g. On the basis of the above results, we can conclude that the S and N atoms are both effectively doped into the 3D graphenic matrix. The contents of doped S and N in m-FeS2@C-SSNFG are measured to be 3.70 at % and 3.94 at %, respectively (Table S1). It is worth noting that the S doping and N doping are beneficial to the electrochemical performance. In detail, co-doping of S and N atoms can not only improve the electronic conductivity due to the stronger electron donor ability of S and N atoms, but also reduce the K+ diffusion barrier owing to the enlarged d-spacing of carbon after doping [41].

The N2 adsorption/desorption experiments were performed to characterize the diverse porous structures. The m-FeS2@C-SSNFG shows the type IV isotherm, suggesting the dominant mesopore structure (Fig. 3h), and the specific surface area (SSA) of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG is measured to be as high as 173.6 m2 g−1 according to the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) theory. In addition, pore width distribution further demonstrates the mesopore nature with pore sizes chiefly centered at 2–5 nm (Fig. 3i). The high surface area in m-FeS2@C-SSNFG will be advantageous to the accessibility of electrolytes and offer abundant active sites for K-ion storage. The loading ratio of m-FeS2 in m-FeS2@C-SSNFG is calculated to be 70.8 wt % by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in air (Fig. S8).

The potassium storage performance of the as-prepared marcasite-FeS2 and m-FeS2@C-SSNFG anodes was investigated in a voltage range of 0.8–3.0 V using 1 M KFSI (Potassium Bis(fluorosulfonyl)imide) in DME (Dimethoxyethane) as the electrolyte. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode were carried out at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 from 0.8 to 3.0 V (Fig. S9). In the initial cathodic scan, the broad peak at around 1.0 V can be attributed to the potassium intercalation to form KxFeS2 (x < 2) [19], corresponding to the slightly inclined plateau from 1.0 to 1.2 V in the initial discharge curve (Fig. 4 a). During the initial anodic scan, the broad peaks near 1.85 and 2.5 V can be ascribed to the deintercalation process of K+ in KxFeS2. Beyond the first cycle, two overlapped couple of redox at 1.1/1.85 V and 1.88/2.5 V can be assigned to the K+ intercalation/deintercalation process in KxFeS2 [19], indicating a good reversibility. The 1st (inset of Figs. 4a), 2nd and 5th, 10th galvanostatic discharge/charge profiles of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode at 0.05 A g−1 are presented in Fig. 4a. The m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode delivers discharge/charge capacities of 590 and 425 mA h g−1 in the first cycle, corresponding to a high initial Coulombic efficiency of 72%. For the pure m-FeS2 electrode with a morphology of particle agglomeration, it only exhibits a low initial charge capacity of 250 mA h g−1, along with a low Coulombic efficiency of 50%, reflecting a poor reversibility (Fig. S10). The relatively high Coulombic efficiency of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode could be attributed to the graphitic carbon shell wrapped around m-FeS2 nanodots, which can protect the m-FeS2 nanodots from forming excessive SEI layer.

Fig. 4b presents the cycling performances of the marcasite-FeS2 and m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrodes at 0.05 A g−1 for 100 cycles. Upon activation of the initial several cycles, a high discharge capacity of 419 mA h g−1 for m-FeS2@C-SSNFG is obtained after 100 cycles. In addition, the Coulombic efficiency quickly raises up to 95% at 10th cycle and is maintained near 100% in the subsequent cycles. For comparison, the cycle stability of pure m-FeS2 was evaluated at 0.05 A g−1. It exhibits fast capacity fading from the initial cycles and only keeps a low capacity of 200 mA h g−1 after 100 cycles, indicating a poor cycle stability. The rate capabilities of the marcasite-FeS2 and m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrodes under different current densities from 0.05 to 5 A g−1 were evaluated in Fig. 4c, demonstrating the favorable effect of highly conductive 3D strutted S, N co-doping graphenic matrix. The m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode delivers high discharge capacities of 460, 400, 358, 312, 269 and 232 mA h g−1 at 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0 and 2.0 A g−1, respectively. Even at a current density of as high as 5 A g−1, a high capacity of 180 mA h g−1 can still be kept. When the current density is set back to 0.05 A g−1, a stable capacity of 423 mA h g−1 is achieved, illustrating good stability under a widespread range of discharge/charge situation. Compared with the previously reported anodes for KIBs (Table S2), the m-FeS2@C-SSNFG shows an exceptional superiority at rate capability (Fig. 4d). However, the rate performance of m-FeS2 electrode is inferior, only exhibiting capacities of 393, 269, 202, 163, 105, 86 and 67 mA h g−1 under 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 and 5 A g−1, respectively. The poor rate capability of m-FeS2 electrode could be attributed to the poor conductivity as well as the severe agglomeration of m-FeS2 nanoparticles.

The long-term cycle stability of the marcasite-FeS2, SSNFG and m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrodes was further measured by the extended charge/discharge tests at 2.0 A g−1 (Fig. 4e and Fig. S11). Obviously, the m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode exhibits superior cycling performance, retaining a high reversible capacity of 175 mA h g−1 with a Coulombic efficiency of ~100% after 1000 long cycles. Contrarily, the fast capacity fading of m-FeS2 electrode can be observed within the initial 200 cycles and a low capacity of only 65 mA h g−1 is maintained after 860 cycles. In addition, the potassium storage performance of SSNFG electrode was also evaluated at 2.0 A g−1. Although it displays a relatively high initial capacity of 255 mA h g−1, the fast capacity drop within the initial 200 cycles can be also observed, with only a reversible capacity of 90 mA h g−1 by 1000th cycle. Particularly, we also note that the cyclic stability of SSNFG electrode is as good as the m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode after 200th cycle. Fig. S12 in the ESM, illustrates and compares the potassiation process of the marcasite-FeS2 and m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrodes as anode materials for KIBs in a voltage range of 0.8–3.0 V and using 1 M KFSI in DME as the electrolyte. For the m-FeS2 electrode, the huge volume changes would lead to agglomeration and pulverization during repeated cycling, triggering to the fast capacity fading (Fig. S13). In return, the m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode shows an outstanding cycle stability, which could be ascribed to the protective microstructures of yolk-shell m-FeS2@graphenic cage structure and core-shell m-FeS2@graphitic carbon structure in 3D graphenic matrix that could alleviate these above problems (Fig. S14). Besides, the m-FeS2 nanodots could decrease the K-intercalation stress due to the multidimensional volume expansion in small particles, improving the cycle stability [4]. In addition to design of microstructure, it is worth underlining that the electrolytes and cut-off voltage also play critical roles to enhance the long-term cycle stability of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG.

For a better understanding of the crucial role of the electrolytes in the cycling stability, we cycled the half-cells in a voltage range of 0.8–3.0 V at 0.5 A g−1 with selected four electrolytes of 1 M KFSI in DME, 1 M KPF6 in DME, 1 M KFSI in EC/DEC and 0.8 M KPF6 in EC/DEC. After the activation upon the first cycle at 0.05 A g−1, the cycling performance of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode with DME-based electrolytes is superior than that of the EC/DEC-based electrolytes, indicating a better chemical stability of DME-based electrolytes (Fig. 5 a and b). Contrarily, the EC/DEC-based electrolytes would react with intermediate polysulfides generated during potassiation/depotassiation of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG, leading to rapid capacity decay (Figs. S15 and S16), which is similar to the reaction between sodium polysulfides and carbonate-based electrolytes [21]. Besides, comparing the effect of two different K salts on cycling stability, we can find that the cycling performance of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode with KFSI salt is more stable than that of KPF6, delivering a high discharge capacity of 300 mA h g−1 after 350 cycles. This excellent cycle stability could be attributed to the highly stable inorganic SEI layer formed on the surface of electrode in KFSI electrolytes and the highly chemical stability of DME-based electrolytes (Fig. S17) [42,43]. Besides, the co-intercalation of solvated K ion in a different solvent may be also important for the excellent cycle stability. The K-carbonate group possesses ponderous volume, suffering from inevitable large volume changes and poor kinetics during charge/discharge process. Contrarily, the optimized DME possesses a 1D flexible chain structure, which would cause smaller volume charges during the intercalation process. In addition, rapid insertion with smaller reaction energy barrier might boost the reaction kinetics between K-DME and the active materials [22].

To further reveal the effect of cut-off voltage on the long-term cycling performance, the half-cells at various voltage windows were cycled in the optimized electrolyte of 1 M KFSI in DME at 5 A g−1. As shown in Fig. 5c, when tested in the voltage window of 0.8–3.0 V, the m-FeS2@C-SSNFG exhibits an unparalleled ultralong cycle stability up to 4000 cycles, retaining a high capacity of 140 mA h g−1 with a Coulombic efficiency of ~100% even at a higher current density of 5 A g−1. When cycled in a voltage range of 0.5–3.0 V, it shows a fairly good cycling performance before 1000th cycle (inset of Fig. 5c), however, the capacity gradually decreases to 100 mA h g−1 after cycling 1500 cycles. Although the capacity of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode cycled in the voltage window of 0.1–3.0 V is higher than that in the voltage range of 0.8–3.0 V initially, it sharply decays after 200th cycle (inset of Fig. 5c) with only a low capacity of 54 mA h g−1 maintained after 1500 cycles. When tested in a conventional electrochemical window of 0.01–3.0 V, it exhibits a same but faster decay trends with a lower capacity of 40 mA h g−1 compared with that of 0.1–3.0 V. It is necessary to point out that the steep capacity increase is observed after the 100th cycle (inset of Fig. 5c), which could be assigned to the decreased particle size caused by the severe volume changes during repeated cycling [44].

Notably, the excellent ultralong cycle stability of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode cycled in the voltage window of 0.8–3.0 V might be closely related to the avoidance of conversion reaction of m-FeS2 during intercalation/deintercalation process by tailoring the cut-off voltage to 0.8 V. To confirm our assumption and further clarify the possible potassium storage mechanism, the ex-situ HRTEM and SAED images of the m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode in different potassiated and depotassiated states were carried out. The HRTEM image and corresponding SAED pattern display lattice fringes with d-spacings of 0.210 nm and 0.260 nm, which are indexed to the (111) plane of metal Fe and the (220) plane of K2S, respectively, indicating the occurrence of conversion reaction after fully discharged to 0.01 V (Fig. 5d and e). Interestingly, after fully discharged to 0.5 V, lattice fringes with interlayer spacings of 0.216 nm can be observed (Fig. 5f and g), which could be assigned to the (134) plane of K1.5FeS2. Meanwhile the product of Fe is also shown (Fig. 5f and g), demonstrating the conversion reaction slightly occurs due to small amounts of overdischarge. When fully discharged to 0.8 V, the observed lattice distance 0.218 nm is assigned to the (240) plane of K1.5FeS2, respectively, revealing the only occurrence of intercalation reaction (Fig. 5h and i). After further charged from 0.8 to 3.0 V, the d-spacing of 0.292 nm can be observed and indexed to the (−221) plane of KFeS2 (Fig. 5j and k), respectively, manifesting that the final charged product is KxFeS2 instead of m-FeS2 [19,21,22].

The ex-situ XRD was used to further confirm the structural changes of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode (Fig. S18). When fully discharged to 0.8 V, the peaks of K1.5FeS2 phase only appear, indicating the only occurrence of intercalation reactions of K+. After being further discharged to 0.5 V, the peaks of K2S and Fe appear, demonstrating the occurrence of conversion reactions from KxFeS2 to Fe and K2S. And when fully discharged to 0.01 V, there are only peaks of Fe, K2S and KCx, and the peaks of Fe are very strong, indicating the fully conversion reactions from KxFeS2 to Fe and K2S. Moreover, when charged from 0.8 V to 3.0 V, there are only peaks of KFeS2 phase, revealing the final charged product is KxFeS2. Besides, the ex-situ XPS analysis of element Fe and S in different potassiated and depotassiated states of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode was also performed to verify the structural changes (Fig. S19), the result of which is consistent with the ex-situ TEM and XRD results. As shown in Fig. S19a after fully potassiated to 0.01 V, the featured peak of metal Fe at 706.8 eV can be only observed, showing the complete occurrence of conversion reaction. When raised the cut-off voltage to 0.5 V, there are two peaks at 707.5 and 706.8 eV, which could be ascribe to the Fe (II) and metal Fe, indicating the co-occurrence of main intercalation reaction and slight conversion reaction. In addition, there is no obvious changes of high-resolution Fe 2p3/2 XPS spectrum when fully discharged to 0.8 V and recharged to 3.0 V, which is due to charge neutrality of KxFeS2 in 0 < x < 2.0 is maintained by S valence variety from (S–S)2− to S2−, instead of the Fe valence variety [45]. The S 2p3/2 XPS spectrum (Fig. S19b) shows the peak at 161.8 eV when discharged to 0.8 V and the peak at 162.5 eV when charged to 3.0 V, respectively, indicating the electrochemical oxidation of S in KxFeS2 during charge process [21]. Moreover, after being fully discharged from 0.8 V to 0.5 V and 0.01 V, the peaks shift to low binding energy indicating the tendency for (S–S)2− to S2− [22]. The peaks centered at 161.2 eV are attributed to the K2S, revealing the occurrence of conversion reactions [46]. Based on above results, we can conclude that the final charged product is KxFeS2.

It is worth highlighting that the KxFeS2 is a conductive phase that is beneficial to the electron/ion transfer, enhancing the rate capability. To prove this, the first-principle calculations were performed. At the beginning of discharging, K ions firstly insert into the octahedral sites of the marcasite FeS2 (Fig. 6 a). With more K ions insertion, at x = 0.5, the FeS6 octahedrons move relativity and K ions occupy the tetrahedral sites, forming the spinel K0.5FeS2 compound with Fd
3 ‾
m space group (Fig. 6b). The FeS6 octahedrons transform into FeS4 tetrahedrons when x ≥ 1, forming KFeS2 and K1.5FeS2 compounds (Fig. 6c and d). And the corresponding optimized lattice constants of FeS2, K0.5FeS2, KFeS2 and K1.5FeS2 unit cells are listed in Table S3 in the ESM. The electronic conductivity of a compound can be evaluated by the calculated electronic structures, including band structure and density of state (DOS). In this work, the band structures and DOS of FeS2 and its three insertion intermediates of KxFeS2 (x = 0.5, 1.0 and 1.5) were calculated in Fig. 6e−l. Fig. 6e and f shows that the electronic states of marcasite FeS2 do not across the Fermi energy level, indicating the marcasite FeS2 is a semiconductor with relatively poor electronic conductivity. After K ions insertion, K0.5FeS2 shows metallic characteristic with considerable electronic states across the Fermi energy level (Fig. 6g and h). When more K ions insert, the metallic characteristic of KxFeS2 (x = 1.0 and 1.5) is enhanced (Fig. 6i−l), indicating a higher electronic conductivity. To further investigate the K ion diffusion behavior in KxFeS2, the K ion diffusion paths (green arrows) in the supercells of KxFeS2 (x = 0, 0.5, 1.0 and 1.5) (Fig. 7 a−d) and corresponding energy variations and energy barriers for K ion diffusion in KxFeS2 (Fig. 7e−g) are calculated. Fig. 7e shows K ion diffusion between two octahedral sites of the marcasite FeS2 has a relatively high activation barrier of 0.703 eV, because the diffusion channels in FeS2 are relatively narrow. The calculated activation barrier of K ion diffusion in the spinel K0.5FeS2 is 0.637 eV, smaller than that of the m-FeS2. For the intermediates of KxFeS2 (x = 1.0 and 1.5) with higher K concentration, the activation barriers of K ion diffusion are calculated to be in the range of 0.563–0.614 eV (Figs. 7f) and 0.539–0.546 eV (Fig. 7g), respectively. Climbing image nudged elastic band (CI-NEB) calculations show the KxFeS2 intermediates have lower activation barriers than the m-FeS2, and the activation barrier basically decreases with the increase of K concentration. Therefore, the KxFeS2 intermediates have better ionic and electronic conductivities than the original m-FeS2. The theoretical results are in line well with our experimental results that the m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode exhibits excellent potassium storage performance in terms of rate capability.

On the basis of these results, the effects of cut-off voltage on the cycling performance of m-FeS2@C nanodots during the intercalation/deintercalation process are illustrated in Fig. S20. As shown in Fig. S20, the m-FeS2 nanodots can maintain its integrity of structure and morphology after long-term cycling when avoiding the conversion reaction. On the contrary, the huge K-intercalation stress caused by conversion reaction would trigger to the pulverization and agglomeration of m-FeS2 nanodots, which leads to the repeated formation of SEI layer and fast capacity fading. The cycling performance of m-FeS2 electrode in different voltage windows were also carried out at 5 A g−1 using 1 M KFSI in DME as the electrolyte (Fig. S21), showing an enhanced cycle stability by tailoring the cut-off voltage of 0.8 V.

The capacity contribution from the capacitive behavior in m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode was also investigated by analyzing the reaction kinetics via CV curves at various scanning rates (Fig. S22a). The peak current (i) is a function of scanning rates (v), which is followed an equation of log(i) = blog(v) + log(a) [47]. Specially, the b-value of 0.5 denotes that the electrochemical reaction is controlled by diffusion process, while the value of 1.0 shows overall control by capacitive process. As for our case, the b-values of cathodic and anodic peaks is 0.85 and 0.88, respectively, revealing the electrochemical system is dominated by both the diffusion-controlled and pseudocapacitive-controlled process (Fig. S22b). Furthermore, based on the relationship of i(V) = k 1 v + k 2 v 1/2, the current (i) at a fixed potential (V) can be divided into capacitive contribution (k 1 v) and diffusion-controlled contribution (k 2 v 1/2) [47]. The ratio of capacitive contribution can be calculated to be 47.9%, 56.5%, 67.3%, 74.4% and 80.4% at different scanning rates of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0 and 2.0 mV s−1 (Fig. S22c). And at a scanning rate of 0.5 mV s−1, the representative CV curve for capacitive current (shaded area) in comparison with whole current is displayed in Fig. S22d. Above results manifest that the high-rate performance of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode is derived from both the intercalation/deintercalation reactions and the capacitive-controlled reactions. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopies (EIS) were further performed to investigate the influence of the 3D strutted S, N co-doping graphene matrix on the electrochemical reaction kinetics process. As shown in Fig. S23, the charge transfer resistance of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode is obviously smaller than that of m-FeS2 and increases slightly after cycling 20 cycles, which demonstrates that the 3D strutted S, N co-doping graphenic matrix can facilitate the K-ion diffusion by forming an 3D electronic network. Moreover, the yolk-shell m-FeS2@graphenic cage structure and the electronic graphitic carbon shell also can together stabilize the m-FeS2 nanodots from agglomeration and keep intimate contact with the m-FeS2 nanodots, leading to small contact resistance. On the contrary, the substantial impedance increase of m-FeS2 after 20 cycles is mainly ascribed to the repeated SEI formation caused by the severe volume expansion and contraction [48].

The potassium storage performance of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode is superior to that of many metal sulfides and carbon-based anode materials in the reported literatures in terms of rate capability and cycle stability (Table S2). Based the above results, the outstanding potassium storage properties of the m-FeS2@C-SSNFG electrode can be attributed to the following factors: (i) The 3D graphenic matrix constructed by the graphenic cages and supported by the robust graphitic struts can not only offer enough interior void space to fully buffer the volume changes of m-FeS2 nanodots during cycling, but also shorten the K+ diffusion length and increase electronic conductivity. Meanwhile the graphitic carbon shell wrapped around m-FeS2 also can confine the active m-FeS2 nanodots in order to avoid agglomeration and dissolution into electrolytes during cycling. (ii) The multi-site S-doping and N-doping enhance the electronic conductivity of the composites and promote the adsorption capability of K+. More importantly, the conductive KxFeS2 possesses higher electronic/K-ion conductivity, boosting the rate capability. (iii) The optimal electrolyte of 1 M KFSI in DME can not only prohibit the side effects of polysulfides on the electrolyte, but also promote the formation of highly stable SEI film [42,43,49]. (iv) The exclusive intercalation/deintercalation reactions of K+ are achieved by tailoring the cut-off voltage of 0.8 V, avoiding the conversion reactions that lead to substantial volume expansion during cycling. The synergetic effects of the advantageous structural design, heteroatoms doping, the optimization of electrolytes and the control of electrochemical reaction process by tailoring the voltage windows jointly contribute to the excellent rate capability and ultralong cycle life. It should be also noted that the original structure of m-FeS2 in m-FeS2@C-SSNFG does not restore after electrochemical cycle, that is, the final charged product is KxFeS2. Nevertheless, compared with the pyrite-FeS2@C-SSNFG anode material, the cycling performance of m-FeS2@C-SSNFG anode material is superior than that of it, demonstrating the usefulness of the m-FeS2@C-SSNFG anode material for KIBs (Fig. S25).

In summary, a unique 3D strutted cell-like S, N co-doped graphenic matrix decorated with marcasite-FeS2@carbon nanodots (m-FeS2@C-SSNFG) composite has been successfully synthesized via a facile chemical blowing method followed by a novel sulfidation strategy. Notably, the semiconductive m-FeS2 transforms into conductive KxFeS2 after first charge process, boosting the electron/K-ion conductivity in the subsequent cycles. Benefiting from the highly conductive and robust structure, the conductive KxFeS2 and the optimized electrolyte and cut-off voltage, the m-FeS2@C-SSNFG presents superior performance of high capacity, outstanding high-rate capability and ultralong cycle stability as an anode material for KIBs. Notably, it exhibits a high capacity of 419 mA h g−1 after 100cycles at 0.05 A g−1, good rate capability (233 and 186 mA h g−1 at 2 and 5 A g−1) and an impressively ultralong cycling performance of 4000 cycles with keeping a high capacity of 140 mA h g−1 even at 5 A g−1. This work opens a new approach to enhance the potassium storage performance for these anodes with severe volume changes.

Kun Han: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Wang Zhao: Investigation, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Qiyao Yu: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Zhiwei Liu: Investigation, Data curation. Ping Li: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Wei (Alex) Wang: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Lei Song: Investigation, Data curation. Fuqiang An: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Peng Cao: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xuanhui Qu: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.
